_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against IDSA 2024 coccidioidomycosis guidelines, published literature on chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis management, and CDC coccidioidomycosis epidemiology data.

h1Title: Chronic Pulmonary Coccidioidomycosis: Symptoms, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis (ICD-10: B38.1) is a persistent Coccidioides immitis or Coccidioides posadasii infection of the lungs and respiratory tract that extends beyond the typical 6–8 week primary infection window, often producing structural lung damage, cavity formation, and chronic symptoms lasting months to years. Unlike primary (acute) coccidioidomycosis, which manifests as a self-limited respiratory infection (Valley fever) in most exposed individuals, chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis develops in approximately 5–10 percent of infected patients and is characterized by persistent pneumonia, lung cavities that may persist or enlarge over time, nodular infiltrates, and progressive fibrotic changes. The condition is endemic to the Southwestern United States (Arizona, California's San Joaquin Valley, southern Nevada, New Mexico, western Texas) and to parts of Mexico and Central/South America, where soil disturbance releases arthroconidia spores that are inhaled into the lungs. Treatment with prolonged antifungal therapy (typically 1–2 years or longer) is the standard of care, and surgical resection may be needed for persistent cavities or complications.

keyStats.prevalence: Coccidioidomycosis (all forms) infects approximately 150,000–200,000 people annually in the US, primarily in endemic regions; chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis develops in approximately 5–10% of those infected, representing an estimated 7,500–20,000 new cases annually
keyStats.demographics: Chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis is more common in men (2:1 male predominance), older adults (median age 50–60 years), and individuals of Filipino, African American, or Native American descent (higher risk of dissemination); no significant geographic predilection beyond endemic areas
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis diagnosis is 50–60 years; primary infection typically occurs in younger individuals (20–40 years) with progression to chronic pulmonary form occurring years later
keyStats.globalCases: Globally, coccidioidomycosis is endemic to the Americas (Southwestern US, northern Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, Venezuela, Argentina, Brazil); estimated 500,000–1,000,000 infections annually worldwide with chronic pulmonary disease in a subset

definition: Chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis (ICD-10: B38.1) is a persistent Coccidioides spp. infection of the lungs characterized by clinical and radiographic evidence of pulmonary involvement lasting more than 3 months, often with structural lung damage including cavities, fibrosis, and bronchiectasis. The primary infection (acute coccidioidomycosis or Valley fever) occurs when arthroconidia (spores) from C. immitis or C. posadasii are inhaled into the lungs, germinate into spherules in the alveolar spaces, and undergo endosporulation (producing endospores that are released to infect additional tissue). In most individuals (60–70%), the primary infection is asymptomatic or produces a mild flu-like illness that resolves spontaneously. In approximately 5–10 percent of patients, the infection persists or reactivates months to years later, manifesting as chronic pulmonary disease with persistent pneumonia, lung cavities (thin-walled or thick-walled), progressive fibrosis, and bronchopleural fistula formation. The term 'chronic' is applied when radiographic changes (cavities, nodules, infiltrates) persist beyond 3 months and symptoms continue or recur. The presence of IgG complement-fixing antibodies at high titers (above 1:16) correlates with more extensive disease and worse prognosis.

typesClassification.type: Chronic coccidioidal pneumonia with persistent infiltrates
typesClassification.description: A persistent pneumonia pattern with ongoing inflammatory infiltrates in the lungs — the patient has symptoms (cough, fever, chest pain, fatigue) and radiographic evidence of infiltrates (often in the upper lobes) that persist beyond 3 months despite or without treatment. May show varying degrees of consolidation, cavitation, and fibrosis. This is the most common form of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis.

A discrete cavitary lesion (or lesions) in the lung that develops during or after primary infection and persists for months to years. Coccidioidal cavities may be thin-walled (asymptomatic, stable) or thick-walled with surrounding inflammation (symptomatic, potentially progressive). Persistent cavities may be a source of ongoing symptoms and can rupture into the pleural space causing pneumothorax or empyema — a serious complication requiring urgent intervention.

A well-defined, rounded granulomatous lesion (granuloma) in the lung representing the chronic localized form of coccidioidomycosis. Coccidioidomas may be single or multiple, are often calcified, and may represent a healed focus of infection with persistent organism. They are generally less symptomatic than cavities or infiltrates but may cause mild symptoms and require monitoring for growth or change.

Advanced chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis with extensive fibrosis, multiple cavities, bronchiectasis, and destroyed lung parenchyma — the late stage of untreated or inadequately treated chronic pulmonary disease. Characterized by significant functional impairment (reduced FEV1, reduced DLCO), chronic productive cough, hemoptysis, and dyspnea. May require lung resection or transplant in extreme cases.

A persistent pleural effusion associated with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis — the effusion may be small and asymptomatic or large and symptomatic. Pleural involvement indicates more extensive disease and raises concern for empyema or bronchopleural fistula. Diagnostic thoracentesis with Coccidioides PCR and culture is required.

primarySymptoms: Persistent cough — a dry or productive cough lasting months, often with minimal sputum; the cough may worsen in the morning and may be exacerbated by physical activity or changes in weather.

Low-grade fever and night sweats — intermittent or persistent low-grade fever (often below 38.5°C), frequently worse at night and accompanied by drenching night sweats that may require changing clothing or bedding; fever may flare during periods of disease activity.

Chest pain — pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worsens with deep breathing or coughing) localized to the area of pulmonary involvement; may be persistent or intermittent; may indicate pleural involvement or cavity expansion.

Fatigue and generalized malaise — profound fatigue disproportionate to the apparent severity of lung disease; interferes with work and daily activities; may improve with effective antifungal treatment.

Weight loss — unintentional weight loss from chronic infection and reduced appetite; may be significant (10–15 lbs in some patients) over months; often accompanied by muscle wasting in advanced disease.

Dyspnea on exertion — shortness of breath with activities that previously did not provoke symptoms; reflects reduced pulmonary function from fibrosis, cavities, or pleural involvement; may worsen over time without treatment.

Hemoptysis (coughing up blood) — small amounts of blood-tinged sputum or frank hemoptysis from cavitary disease eroding into blood vessels; more common in fibrocavitary disease; any hemoptysis warrants evaluation as it may indicate cavity rupture or bronchiectasis.

Wheezing and respiratory symptoms — airway involvement from chronic inflammation causes wheezing and non-specific respiratory symptoms that may be confused with asthma or COPD; Coccidioides can cause obstructive airway disease as well as restrictive disease.

Renal or other extrapulmonary involvement — in some patients with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis, the infection disseminates to other organs (skin, bones and joints, meninges, prostate) causing additional symptoms (skin nodules, bone pain, headache); dissemination is more common in immunocompromised patients.

Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic course — some patients with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis (particularly those with stable thin-walled cavities or coccidioidomas) may be relatively asymptomatic, discovered incidentally on chest imaging performed for other reasons; the disease may be stable for years in some patients.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent cough and pulmonary symptoms lasting more than 6 weeks following travel to or residence in an endemic area (Southwestern US, northern Mexico)

New or worsening infiltrates on chest X-ray more than 6 weeks after initial coccidioidal pneumonia — persisting or worsening infiltrates suggest chronic pulmonary disease rather than resolving primary infection

Development of lung cavities or nodules on imaging in a patient with prior coccidioidal pneumonia — cavities appearing during or after primary infection indicate the beginning of chronic pulmonary disease

Elevated or rising complement-fixing (CF) antibody titers more than 6 weeks after primary infection — high CF titers (above 1:16) correlate with more extensive pulmonary disease and risk of progression

Symptoms of coccidioidal dissemination (skin lesions, bone pain, headache) in a patient with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis — dissemination indicates worsening immunological containment of the infection
emergencySigns: Massive hemoptysis — coughing up large amounts of blood (more than 300–500 mL in 24 hours) from a cavitary lesion eroding into a pulmonary artery; requires immediate evaluation in an emergency setting with bronchovascular isolation if possible

Pneumothorax (sudden chest pain with dyspnea) — cavity rupture into the pleural space causes spontaneous pneumothorax, a recognized complication of coccidioidal cavities; requires urgent chest tube placement

Pleural empyema from cavity rupture — rupture of a coccidioidal cavity into the pleural space can cause a pyopneumothorax (pus and air in the pleural space); requires urgent chest tube drainage and antifungal treatment

Coccidioidal meningitis with new neurological symptoms — headache, stiff neck, confusion, or cranial nerve palsies in a patient with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis indicate possible meningeal spread; meningitis is nearly universally fatal without treatment and requires urgent antifungal therapy

Acute worsening of dyspnea with new hypoxia — may indicate progression of pulmonary disease, development of pleural effusion, or cavity expansion with mass effect; requires urgent imaging and evaluation

causes.description: The primary infection begins when arthroconidia (spores) from Coccidioides spp. in soil are inhaled into the alveolar spaces. In endemic areas, soil disturbance (construction, agriculture, dust storms, archaeology digs) releases arthroconidia into the air. Once inhaled, arthroconidia germinate into spherules (20–100 microns in diameter) that undergo endosporulation (producing 100–200 endospores per spherule), which are released to infect additional lung tissue. This cycle causes the primary pneumonia.

causes.cause: Inadequate immune containment of primary infection
causes.description: Most immunocompetent individuals successfully contain primary coccidioidomycosis through cell-mediated immunity (activated macrophages, CD4+ T-helper 1 cells, cytokine-mediated granuloma formation). Chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis develops when this immune containment is incomplete — either due to host immune defects (HIV, TNF-alpha inhibitor use, corticosteroids, organ transplant immunosuppression) or high inoculum exposure that overwhelms the immune response. In some patients, no obvious immune defect is identified, suggesting individual variability in immune response.

Coccidioides can persist in a dormant form (likely within granulomas) after primary infection resolves. Reactivation of dormant organisms months to years later can cause a new pulmonary infection that presents as chronic disease. This is analogous to reactivation TB and is more likely to occur when host immunity wanes (aging, immunosuppression).

High-dose exposure to Coccidioides arthroconidia (such as during archaeological digs, mining operations, or dust storms in endemic areas) can cause more severe primary infection with a higher likelihood of progressing to chronic pulmonary disease. The inoculum size influences the severity of primary infection and the likelihood of chronic sequelae.

causes.cause: Re-infection with a different Coccidioides species or strain
causes.description: Although rare, re-infection with Coccidioides (different species or strain) can occur in endemic areas, presenting as new or recurrent pulmonary coccidioidomycosis in a patient who has previously had the infection. This can be difficult to distinguish from reactivation and requires molecular typing of the isolate for confirmation.

Patients with pre-existing pulmonary disease (COPD, asthma, bronchiectasis, prior TB) are more likely to develop chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis — the damaged lung parenchyma provides a more favorable environment for Coccidioides persistence and progression. Structural lung damage also makes antifungal penetration more difficult.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to endemic areas
riskFactors.description: The endemic regions for Coccidioides include the Southwestern US (Arizona, California's San Joaquin Valley, southern Nevada, New Mexico, western Texas), northern Mexico, and parts of Central and South America. Living or working in these areas, especially in areas with dusty soil, dramatically increases the risk of acquiring coccidioidomycosis.

riskFactors.factor: Immunosuppression (HIV, TNF-alpha inhibitors, organ transplant, corticosteroids)
riskFactors.description: Immunosuppression dramatically increases the risk of progression to chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis and dissemination. HIV/AIDS with low CD4 counts, TNF-alpha inhibitors (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept), organ transplant immunosuppression, and systemic corticosteroid use all increase risk. Patients on these medications who develop coccidioidomycosis require more aggressive treatment.

riskFactors.factor: African American, Filipino, or Native American ethnicity
riskFactors.description: African Americans, Filipinos, and Native Americans have a higher risk of disseminated coccidioidomycosis (2–5 times higher than the general population) and likely also higher risk of chronic pulmonary disease. The biological basis for this disparity is not fully understood but may involve differences in immune response or vitamin D metabolism.

Pregnancy, particularly the third trimester, is associated with increased risk of coccidioidomycosis progression and dissemination — immune modulation during pregnancy reduces cell-mediated immunity and allows Coccidioides to proliferate. Pregnant patients with coccidioidomycosis require close monitoring.

riskFactors.factor: Severe primary coccidioidal pneumonia with high CF titers
riskFactors.description: Primary coccidioidal pneumonia with complement-fixing antibody titers above 1:16, widespread infiltrates, or requiring hospitalization indicates more severe primary infection with a higher likelihood of chronic pulmonary sequelae. These patients warrant close follow-up with repeat imaging.

Patients with structural lung disease are more susceptible to chronic Coccidioides infection. Optimizing management of underlying lung disease (inhaled bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation) may reduce the risk of chronic coccidioidomycosis.

affectedDemographics: Chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis predominantly affects adults aged 40–70 years who were likely infected in their 20s or 30s, with the chronic pulmonary manifestation developing years later

Male-to-female ratio is approximately 2:1, possibly reflecting occupational exposure differences (more men in construction, agriculture, military in endemic areas) and hormonal influences on immune response
African Americans, Filipinos, and Native Americans have elevated risk of severe and disseminated coccidioidomycosis, including chronic pulmonary forms

Immunocompromised individuals (HIV, transplant recipients, TNF-alpha inhibitor users) are overrepresented among chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis cases, reflecting the importance of cell-mediated immunity in controlling the infection
Residents of the Southwestern US endemic regions (Arizona, California's San Joaquin Valley) account for the majority of cases in the US
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis requires integration of clinical findings (persistent respiratory symptoms), radiographic evidence (persistent infiltrates, cavities, or nodules beyond 3 months), and laboratory confirmation of Coccidioides infection. The diagnostic pathway begins with serologic testing: enzyme immunoassay (EIA) for IgM and IgG Coccidioides antibodies provides initial screening (positive in 70–80% of chronic pulmonary cases), while complement fixation (CF) titers quantify the severity of infection (higher titers correlate with more extensive disease). Coccidioides urinary antigen testing is useful for disseminated disease. Sputum culture and PCR (from sputum, BAL, or tissue) provide definitive identification of Coccidioides. Imaging (chest X-ray, CT chest) demonstrates the characteristic pulmonary findings. For difficult-to-diagnose cases, bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) for culture and PCR, or CT-guided lung biopsy, may be required.

diagnosticTests.test: Coccidioides serology — EIA and complement fixation (CF)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Serologic testing is the first-line diagnostic tool for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis — EIA detects IgM (early infection, positive within 1–3 weeks) and IgG (later, persists longer); CF titers quantify disease severity (higher titers indicate more extensive disease). EIA is sensitive (70–80%) but CF is more specific and useful for monitoring treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; EIA results in 2–5 days; CF titers in 7–14 days. CF is performed by reference laboratories (Mayo, ARUP). Rising CF titers indicate progression; falling titers indicate response to treatment.

diagnosticTests.test: Coccidioides PCR from sputum or bronchoscopy specimen
diagnosticTests.purpose: PCR testing of respiratory specimens (sputum, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, lung biopsy tissue) provides rapid, specific identification of Coccidioides DNA. PCR is more sensitive than culture for detecting Coccidioides in clinical specimens and faster than culture (results in 1–3 days vs. 3–7 days).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sputum collection (early morning, deep cough) or bronchoscopy with BAL. Sent for PCR testing at reference laboratories. Results in 1–5 days depending on the lab.

diagnosticTests.test: Sputum or BAL culture for Coccidioides
diagnosticTests.purpose: Culture of respiratory specimens is the gold standard for definitive diagnosis — growth of Coccidioides spp. confirms the diagnosis. Culture also allows species identification (C. immitis vs. C. posadasii) and antifungal susceptibility testing. However, culture takes 3–7 days and requires biosafety level 3 containment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sputum (deep cough, early morning), BAL fluid, or lung tissue. Culture at specialized reference labs with BSL-3 facilities. Positive in 50–70% of chronic pulmonary cases. Takes 3–7 days for growth.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Urinary antigen testing detects Coccidioides galactomannan and is most useful for disseminated disease (80–90% sensitivity) but can also be positive in severe chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis. Used when serology is negative but clinical suspicion is high, particularly in immunocompromised patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urine sample; results in 2–5 days. Sensitivity is higher in disseminated disease than in isolated pulmonary disease. Available from reference laboratories (Mayo, MiraVista).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Imaging demonstrates the structural lung damage characteristic of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis: persistent infiltrates (often upper lobe), thin-walled or thick-walled cavities, nodules (coccidioidomas), fibrosis, bronchiectasis, and pleural thickening. CT is more sensitive than chest X-ray for detecting early cavities, nodules, and pleural involvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Chest X-ray is fast and cheap; CT chest provides more detailed information. Both are non-invasive. CT takes 15–20 minutes. IV contrast may be used for better visualization of mediastinal structures.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Bronchoscopy allows direct visualization of the airways, collection of BAL fluid for culture, PCR, and cytology, and transbronchial biopsy if needed. Indicated when sputum studies are non-diagnostic but clinical suspicion remains high, or when the diagnosis of malignancy vs. coccidioidomycosis is in question.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Moderate sedation; flexible bronchoscope passed through the nose or mouth into the airways. BAL fluid collected from the affected lung segment. Takes 20–30 minutes. Discomfort for 1–2 days afterward. Risk includes minor bleeding and very rarely pneumothorax.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Lung biopsy (percutaneous CT-guided core biopsy or video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery/VATS) provides tissue for histopathology (visualizing spherules), culture, and PCR. Indicated when other diagnostic methods are non-diagnostic or when malignancy must be excluded in a patient with a lung mass.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT-guided biopsy: local anesthetic, needle passed through chest wall into the lesion; takes 30–45 minutes. VATS: general anesthesia, small incisions; longer recovery. Risks include pneumothorax (10–15%), bleeding. Histopathology results in 3–5 days; culture in 5–14 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis requires prolonged antifungal therapy, typically for 1–2 years or longer, with the goal of achieving clinical and radiographic stability and preventing further lung damage. The first-line treatment is oral fluconazole (400–800 mg daily) or itraconazole (200 mg twice daily) — both are effective for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis and are better tolerated than the historical treatment (amphotericin B). Fluconazole has better CNS penetration and is preferred when meningeal involvement is possible. Itraconazole has slightly better pulmonary penetration and is sometimes preferred for isolated pulmonary disease. For more severe or refractory cases, voriconazole, posaconazole, or isavuconazole (newer azoles with broader spectrum) may be used. In severe disease unresponsive to oral azoles, amphotericin B (conventional or liposomal) may be used for induction, followed by oral azole maintenance. Surgery (lung resection or cavity excision) is indicated for persistent cavities causing recurrent hemoptysis, suspected malignancy not ruled out by biopsy, or cavity rupture into the pleural space.

medicalTreatments.name: Fluconazole (first-line azole for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis)
medicalTreatments.description: Fluconazole (400–800 mg orally daily) is the first-line treatment for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis — it is effective, well-tolerated, and has excellent CNS penetration (critical if meningeal spread occurs). Standard dose is 400 mg daily; higher doses (600–800 mg) are used for more severe disease. Treatment duration is typically 1–2 years, often longer, until clinical stability and radiographic improvement are achieved.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response rates of 60–80% for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis. Symptoms improve within 4–8 weeks in most responders; radiographic improvement may take 6–12 months. Treatment is often continued for 1–2 years after clinical stabilization, and some patients require indefinite maintenance therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole (alternative first-line azole)
medicalTreatments.description: Itraconazole (200 mg orally twice daily) is an alternative first-line agent for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis, particularly when fluconazole is not preferred. Itraconazole has somewhat better pulmonary tissue penetration than fluconazole. It requires acidic gastric environment for absorption and should be taken with food; serum levels should be monitored to ensure adequate absorption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Similar efficacy to fluconazole for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis (60–75% response rate). Monitoring of serum itraconazole levels (target trough above 1–2 µg/mL) is recommended to ensure therapeutic levels. Side effects include GI upset, hepatotoxicity, and negative inotropic effect (caution in heart failure).

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole (second-line azole for refractory disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Voriconazole (4 mg/kg IV every 12 hours, then 200 mg orally twice daily) is used for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis that fails to respond to fluconazole or itraconazole, or for patients with severe disease requiring rapid antifungal activity. Voriconazole is more potent against Coccidioides than fluconazole.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in fluconazole-refractory cases; response rates of 70–80% in refractory chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis. Significant side effects include hepatotoxicity, visual disturbances (40% of patients), and neurotoxicity (hallucinations, confusion) at high levels. Requires therapeutic drug monitoring.

medicalTreatments.name: Posaconazole (newer triazole for refractory or intolerant disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Posaconazole (delayed-release tablet 300 mg twice daily on day 1, then 300 mg daily) is a newer broad-spectrum triazole used for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis refractory to other azoles or in patients who cannot tolerate other antifungals. It has excellent activity against Coccidioides and is generally well tolerated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Promising efficacy in case series for refractory coccidioidomycosis (including pulmonary disease); approved for prophylaxis in immunocompromised patients. Consider for salvage therapy. Side effects are generally mild (GI upset). Does not require food for absorption (unlike itraconazole).

medicalTreatments.name: Amphotericin B (for severe disease unresponsive to oral azoles)
medicalTreatments.description: Amphotericin B deoxycholate (0.7–1.0 mg/kg IV daily) or liposomal amphotericin B (3–5 mg/kg IV daily) is used for severe chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis that does not respond to oral azoles, or as initial therapy in patients who are too ill to take oral medications. Amphotericin B has rapid fungicidal activity against Coccidioides.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for severe disease but less commonly needed with effective oral azoles available. Amphotericin B requires IV infusion and has significant toxicity (nephrotoxicity, electrolyte abnormalities, infusion reactions). Liposomal formulations have less nephrotoxicity but are more expensive.

medicalTreatments.name: Adjunctive corticosteroids (for severe inflammation)
medicalTreatments.description: Short courses of corticosteroids (prednisone 20–40 mg daily tapering over 2–4 weeks) may be used as adjunctive therapy in chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis with severe inflammatory symptoms (marked dyspnea, fever, pleuritic pain) not controlled by antifungal therapy alone. Corticosteroids reduce inflammation but do not treat the infection itself — they must always be combined with antifungals.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adjunctive corticosteroids can rapidly improve symptoms in patients with severe inflammatory response; they do not substitute for antifungal therapy and should be used cautiously given the risk of immunosuppression. Short courses are generally safe when combined with adequate antifungal coverage.

surgicalOptions.name: Lung resection for persistent cavity or suspected malignancy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical resection of coccidioidal cavities (segmentectomy, lobectomy) is indicated for persistent cavities causing recurrent hemoptysis, cavities with suspected malignancy not ruled out by biopsy, or large cavities with ongoing symptoms unresponsive to antifungal therapy. The goal is to remove damaged, non-functional lung tissue and prevent complications.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical resection of coccidioidal cavities achieves symptomatic relief in most patients with recurrent hemoptysis; recurrence of coccidioidomycosis in the remaining lung is possible and may require ongoing antifungal therapy. Surgical mortality is 1–3% in experienced centers.

surgicalOptions.name: Decortication for empyema or trapped lung
surgicalOptions.description: When coccidioidal pleural disease causes thick pleural peel formation (empyema, fibrotic 'trapped' lung that cannot re-expand), decortication (surgical removal of the pleural peel) can allow lung re-expansion and improve respiratory function. Indicated for chronic pleural thickening causing symptomatic restriction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Decortication can significantly improve respiratory function in selected patients with pleural peel and trapped lung; outcomes depend on the reversibility of lung restriction. Mortality 2–5% in experienced centers.

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation (for end-stage fibrocavitary disease)
surgicalOptions.description: Lung transplantation may be considered for patients with end-stage fibrocavitary coccidioidomycosis causing severe respiratory failure (FEV1 below 25% predicted, refractory hypoxemia) who have failed all other treatments. Transplantation for coccidioidomycosis is technically feasible but requires lifelong antifungal therapy to prevent recurrence in the transplanted lung.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Lung transplant for coccidioidomycosis has good medium-term outcomes (1-year survival 70–80%, 5-year survival 50–60%) in carefully selected patients. Recurrence of coccidioidomycosis in the transplanted lung is possible but manageable with antifungal prophylaxis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Observation without treatment (for stable, asymptomatic chronic disease)
alternativeTreatments.description: Some patients with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis (particularly those with stable thin-walled cavities or coccidioidomas and no symptoms) may be managed with close observation rather than immediate antifungal treatment. Serial imaging and clinical monitoring every 3–6 months identifies progression. Treatment is initiated if symptoms develop, cavities enlarge, or CF titers rise.

alternativeTreatments.name: Inhaled amphotericin B (investigational)
alternativeTreatments.description: Inhaled liposomal amphotericin B has been studied as adjunctive therapy for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis — delivering antifungal directly to the lungs. Evidence is limited but may be considered in refractory cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Immunomodulation (IFN-gamma, G-CSF)
alternativeTreatments.description: Adjunctive immunotherapy (interferon-gamma, granulocyte colony-stimulating factor) has been explored in refractory coccidioidomycosis to enhance cell-mediated immune response. Evidence is limited to case reports and small series.

whySeeSpecialist: Chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis requires management by an infectious disease specialist with expertise in endemic mycoses, working in conjunction with a pulmonologist for pulmonary management. Chronic antifungal therapy requires monitoring for toxicity, drug interactions, and treatment response. Patients with severe disease, cavities, or complications may need cardiothoracic surgery involvement.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease specialist with experience managing coccidioidomycosis — ideally at a medical center in an endemic region (Arizona, California) where they see many cases. Ask whether they follow IDSA coccidioidomycosis treatment guidelines, whether they monitor serum antifungal levels (for fluconazole at high doses, voriconazole, itraconazole), and whether they have access to newer azoles (posaconazole, isavuconazole).
preventionStrategies: Avoidance of high-risk activities in endemic areas — minimizing soil disturbance (construction, gardening) during dust storms, using proper respiratory protection (N95 mask) when working in dusty soil in endemic areas
Dust control measures — wetting soil before disturbance, using air filtration in high-risk occupational settings (mining, archaeology, construction) in endemic areas reduces arthroconidia exposure
Pre-travel counseling for immunocompromised individuals — immunocompromised individuals traveling to endemic areas should receive counseling about coccidioidomycosis risk and prevention measures
No effective pre-exposure prophylaxis — there is no vaccine for coccidioidomycosis; prevention relies on exposure reduction
Early recognition and treatment of primary coccidioidomycosis — early antifungal treatment of primary infection in high-risk patients may reduce the risk of progression to chronic pulmonary disease
lifestyleModifications: Respiratory protection in endemic areas — using N95 or higher respirators when working in dusty environments (construction, farming) in endemic regions, especially during dust storms

Avoiding extensive soil disturbance in endemic areas — limiting activities that disturb large amounts of soil (construction, landscaping) during dry, dusty seasons when arthroconidia are most airborne
Staying indoors during dust storms — dust storms in endemic areas can carry high concentrations of arthroconidia; staying inside with windows closed during dust storms reduces exposure
Avoiding smoking — smoking worsens chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis by impairing local pulmonary defenses; smoking cessation improves outcomes
Adequate rest and nutrition during treatment — antifungal treatment can be prolonged; adequate rest and nutrition support the immune system's contribution to controlling the infection

recommended: High-protein diet to support immune function and tissue repair — lean meats, fish, eggs, legumes, dairy; protein intake of 1.0–1.2 g/kg/day

Balanced diet with adequate fruits and vegetables — provides vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants supporting immune function; particularly important in endemic areas where overall health affects susceptibility
Adequate hydration — especially important when using azole antifungals (which may be hepatotoxic); adequate fluid intake supports liver function and overall health
avoid: Alcohol during fluconazole or itraconazole treatment — alcohol stresses the liver already processing azole antifungals, increasing hepatotoxicity risk
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice — grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4, which metabolizes itraconazole and voriconazole, potentially increasing their toxicity; fluconazole is not significantly affected
High-fat meals before itraconazole dosing — itraconazole absorption requires an acidic environment and is enhanced by food; take with a full meal as directed
Excessive vitamin A supplementation — very high doses of vitamin A may interact with antifungal therapy; normal dietary intake is acceptable

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise (walking, gentle swimming) is beneficial for overall health during chronic coccidioidomycosis treatment. However, intense exercise may worsen symptoms in patients with advanced fibrocavitary disease or limited pulmonary reserve. Patients with significant dyspnea should engage in low-impact activities. Pulmonary rehabilitation may help patients with advanced chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis.
dailyManagement: Daily antifungal medication adherence — fluconazole or itraconazole must be taken consistently every day; missed doses reduce efficacy and increase risk of resistance; setting a daily reminder helps maintain adherence

Serum antifungal level monitoring (if on high-dose fluconazole, voriconazole, or itraconazole) — therapeutic drug monitoring ensures adequate levels and prevents toxicity; levels checked 1–2 weeks after starting treatment and after any dose change

Liver function monitoring — azole antifungals are hepatotoxic; LFTs checked at baseline, 2 weeks, 4 weeks, then every 3 months during treatment; report any signs of liver dysfunction (jaundice, dark urine, right upper quadrant pain) immediately
Monthly symptom review — tracking cough, fever, weight, dyspnea, and hemoptysis helps assess treatment response; any worsening warrants evaluation
Repeat imaging every 6–12 months — chest X-ray or CT to assess treatment response, monitor cavity size, and detect new lesions; imaging should be compared to prior studies
CF titer monitoring — CF titers should be monitored every 3–6 months to assess treatment response; rising titers suggest inadequate treatment or progression
prognosis: The prognosis of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis depends on the extent of lung damage at diagnosis, the patient's immune status, and the adequacy of treatment. Patients with limited chronic pulmonary disease (single cavity, focal infiltrates) treated with appropriate antifungal therapy have a good prognosis — 60–80% achieve clinical stabilization and symptom improvement. Patients with advanced fibrocavitary disease or extensive lung damage have a worse prognosis — ongoing symptoms, declining pulmonary function, and complications (hemoptysis, cavity rupture) are common despite treatment. Lifelong antifungal maintenance therapy may be required in some patients with extensive disease. Dissemination to the meninges (coccidioidal meningitis) is the most serious complication and requires indefinite treatment to prevent death.
recoveryTimeline: Clinical improvement typically occurs within 4–8 weeks of effective antifungal treatment — cough decreases, fever resolves, energy improves. Radiographic improvement (reduction in cavity size, infiltrates, or fibrosis) is slower and may take 6–12 months to become apparent. Many patients require 1–2 years of treatment before clinical and radiographic stability is achieved; some require longer or indefinite maintenance therapy. Complete resolution of cavities and fibrosis is uncommon — the goal is clinical stability and prevention of further damage rather than full radiographic cure.
complications: Coccidioidal cavity rupture — thin-walled cavities can rupture into the pleural space, causing pneumothorax (air in the pleural space) or pyopneumothorax (air plus infected fluid); both require urgent chest tube drainage and antifungal treatment; mortality from cavity rupture is approximately 10–20%

Massive hemoptysis — chronic cavities can erode into pulmonary arteries, causing life-threatening hemoptysis; management includes bronchial artery embolization and, rarely, surgical resection; mortality from massive hemoptysis is approximately 10–15%

Bronchiectasis — chronic coccidioidomycosis can cause permanent bronchial dilation (bronchiectasis) leading to chronic productive cough, recurrent respiratory infections, and progressive respiratory decline

Coccidioidal dissemination (skin, bone, meninges) — when the immune system fails to contain Coccidioides, the infection spreads to other organs; dissemination is more common in immunocompromised patients and those with high CF titers; meningitis is the most serious form of dissemination with near-100% mortality without treatment

Secondary bacterial pneumonia — patients with chronic coccidioidal cavities and bronchiectasis are at increased risk for bacterial superinfection (Pseudomonas, Staphylococcus) causing acute worsening of respiratory symptoms; requires additional antibiotic coverage

Lung fibrosis and restrictive lung disease — chronic coccidioidomycosis causes progressive pulmonary fibrosis, reducing lung volumes and gas exchange; advanced fibrosis may cause chronic hypoxemia requiring supplemental oxygen

supportResources.name: Valley Fever Center for Excellence (Arizona)
supportResources.description: Arizona-based center of excellence for coccidioidomycosis research, education, and clinical care. Resources for patients and physicians.

supportResources.name: CDC Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever) Information
supportResources.description: CDC resources on coccidioidomycosis epidemiology, diagnosis, and prevention.

supportResources.name: IDSA Coccidioidomycosis Guidelines 2024
supportResources.description: Evidence-based clinical practice guidelines for coccidioidomycosis diagnosis and treatment from the Infectious Diseases Society of America.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB and chronic coccidioidomycosis have similar presentations (chronic cough, weight loss, upper lobe cavities) and both are endemic in some of the same geographic regions (Southwestern US). Key differences: TB has acid-fast bacilli on sputum smear, positive IGRA/T-spot, and cavity walls are often thicker than coccidioidal cavities; TB has a different treatment (RIPE therapy). Both can coexist. Coccidioides serology (EIA, CF) and culture distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary Lung Cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lung cancer can present as a lung mass or nodule that may be confused with coccidioidoma. Key differences: coccidioidomycosis has positive coccidioides serology and typically affects both lungs over time; cancer presents as a solitary nodule/mass, often with smoking history. Lung biopsy may be needed to distinguish them when the diagnosis is uncertain.

confusedWithConditions.name: Coccidioidal Meningitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Coccidioidal meningitis is dissemination of Coccidioides to the CNS — it can occur in patients with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis but is a separate clinical entity. The presence of headache, stiff neck, and neurological symptoms indicates meningeal involvement requiring urgent antifungal therapy with fluconazole (which crosses the blood-brain barrier) and possibly intrathecal amphotericin B.

relatedConditions.name: Primary Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever)
relatedConditions.relevance: Primary coccidioidomycosis (acute Valley fever) is the initial infection from which chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis develops in approximately 5–10% of patients. Primary infection is typically self-limited and milder; chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis represents the progression to persistent, symptomatic, structural lung damage.

relatedConditions.name: Coccidioidal Meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Coccidioidal meningitis is the most serious complication of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis — it occurs when the infection spreads from the lungs to the meninges via the bloodstream. Any patient with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis with new headache or neurological symptoms must be evaluated for meningitis with lumbar puncture.

relatedConditions.name: Disseminated Coccidioidomycosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Disseminated coccidioidomycosis (skin nodules, bone and joint involvement) represents hematogenous spread of Coccidioides from the pulmonary focus. Patients with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis and high CF titers or immunosuppression are at risk for dissemination and require monitoring for skin, bone, and other organ involvement.

faqs.question: What is chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: Chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis (ICD-10: B38.1) is a persistent infection of the lungs by Coccidioides (C. immitis or C. posadasii) that causes structural lung damage — including cavities, infiltrates, fibrosis — lasting more than 3 months. It develops in approximately 5–10% of people infected with coccidioidomycosis (Valley fever) and is characterized by persistent cough, fever, chest pain, weight loss, and fatigue. Treatment requires prolonged antifungal therapy (1–2 years or longer).

faqs.question: What is the difference between acute and chronic coccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: Acute coccidioidomycosis (Valley fever) is the initial self-limited infection from inhaling Coccidioides spores — it causes flu-like symptoms (fever, cough, fatigue) that usually resolve within 6–8 weeks without treatment in most people. Chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis is the persistent form that develops in some patients months to years after primary infection, causing ongoing lung damage (cavities, fibrosis) and symptoms that require antifungal treatment.

faqs.question: Where is coccidioidomycosis found?
faqs.answer: Coccidioidomycosis is endemic to the Southwestern United States — particularly Arizona, California's San Joaquin Valley, southern Nevada, New Mexico, and western Texas. It is also endemic to northern Mexico and parts of Central and South America (Guatemala, Honduras, Venezuela, Argentina, Brazil). The fungus lives in desert soil, and soil disturbance releases spores into the air.

faqs.question: How is chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis involves: (1) clinical findings (persistent cough, fever, weight loss) for more than 3 months; (2) chest imaging (X-ray or CT showing cavities, infiltrates, or nodules that persist); (3) laboratory confirmation — Coccidioides serology (EIA and complement fixation titers), sputum culture or PCR. CF titers above 1:16 indicate more extensive disease. Bronchoscopy or lung biopsy may be needed if non-diagnostic.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: Treatment is prolonged antifungal therapy — oral fluconazole (400–800 mg daily) or itraconazole (200 mg twice daily) for 1–2 years or longer. Fluconazole is preferred because it has excellent CNS penetration (important if meningitis develops). Monitoring of serum levels, liver function, and CF titers guides treatment duration. Some patients require indefinite maintenance therapy.

faqs.question: Can chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Many patients with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis can be effectively managed with antifungal therapy, achieving clinical stability and symptom improvement. However, structural lung damage (cavities, fibrosis) often persists even after successful treatment — the goal is often clinical control rather than complete radiographic cure. Some patients require lifelong maintenance antifungal therapy to prevent relapse.

faqs.question: Is coccidioidomycosis contagious?
faqs.answer: Coccidioidomycosis is not contagious from person to person — it is acquired by inhaling spores from the environment (soil). There is no human-to-human transmission. Patients with coccidioidomycosis do not need to be isolated from family members or other patients.

faqs.question: What are the complications of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: Complications include: cavity rupture causing pneumothorax or empyema (requires urgent chest tube drainage); massive hemoptysis (requires bronchial artery embolization or surgery); bronchiectasis (chronic productive cough, recurrent infections); coccidioidal meningitis (headache, stiff neck, neurological symptoms — requires urgent treatment); dissemination to skin, bones, and joints; progressive pulmonary fibrosis and respiratory failure.

faqs.question: How long must treatment be continued?
faqs.answer: Treatment of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis is typically continued for 1–2 years after achieving clinical stability (resolution of symptoms, stable or improving imaging). Many patients require longer treatment, and some need indefinite maintenance therapy to prevent relapse. The decision to stop treatment is based on clinical response, radiographic improvement, and falling CF titers.

faqs.question: What is a coccidioidal cavity?
faqs.answer: A coccidioidal cavity is a hollow space within the lung caused by coccidioidomycosis — it forms as the infection destroys lung tissue, leaving a cavity. Coccidioidal cavities may be thin-walled (more stable, less symptomatic) or thick-walled (more active, associated with ongoing infection). Persistent cavities can cause hemoptysis, rupture into the pleural space (causing pneumothorax), or become a site of bacterial superinfection.

faqs.question: Can surgery remove coccidioidal cavities?
faqs.answer: Yes — surgery (segmentectomy or lobectomy) can remove persistent coccidioidal cavities, particularly those causing recurrent hemoptysis, those suspicious for malignancy, or large cavities that do not respond to antifungal therapy. Surgery is also needed for cavity rupture causing pneumothorax or empyema. However, surgery is not a substitute for antifungal therapy — antifungals are continued pre- and post-operatively.

faqs.question: What is the risk of coccidioidal meningitis?
faqs.answer: Coccidioidal meningitis is a complication of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis — the infection spreads from the lungs to the meninges via the bloodstream. It occurs in approximately 1–5% of patients with coccidioidomycosis and is nearly universally fatal without treatment. Any patient with coccidioidomycosis who develops headache, stiff neck, or confusion should have an urgent lumbar puncture to rule out meningitis.

faqs.question: Does coccidioidomycosis cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: Yes — chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis causes permanent structural lung damage including cavities, fibrosis, and bronchiectasis. This damage does not fully reverse even with successful treatment. The goal of treatment is to prevent further damage and achieve clinical stability. Patients with advanced damage may have permanent respiratory impairment requiring ongoing management.

faqs.question: What raises the risk of chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: Risk factors include: immunosuppression (HIV, TNF-alpha inhibitors, organ transplant, steroids); African American, Filipino, or Native American ethnicity (higher risk of severe disease); residence in endemic areas; severe primary coccidioidal pneumonia; pre-existing lung disease (COPD, bronchiectasis); pregnancy; high inoculum exposure (dust storms, construction in endemic areas).

faqs.question: Can coccidioidomycosis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes — relapse after stopping antifungal therapy occurs in approximately 10–20% of patients with chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis. Relapse is more common in patients with high CF titers at treatment completion, extensive lung damage, or immunosuppression. Many patients require long-term or indefinite maintenance therapy to prevent relapse. Any recurrence of symptoms warrants re-evaluation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between coccidioidomycosis and Valley fever?
faqs.answer: Valley fever is another name for coccidioidomycosis — the name comes from the San Joaquin Valley in California, where it was first described and remains highly endemic. Valley fever typically refers to the acute (primary) form of the infection. Chronic pulmonary coccidioidomycosis is the persistent, progressive form that develops in some patients months to years later.

faqs.question: How is Coccidioides transmitted?
faqs.answer: Coccidioides is not transmitted person-to-person — it is acquired by inhaling arthroconidia (spores) from environmental soil. The fungus grows in desert soil in endemic regions. When soil is disturbed (construction, farming, dust storms, archaeology), spores become airborne and are inhaled into the lungs, where they germinate and cause infection.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for coccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: There is no commercially available vaccine for coccidioidomycosis. Research into coccidioidomycosis vaccines is ongoing but no candidate has reached clinical use. Prevention relies on exposure reduction (respiratory protection, avoiding soil disturbance in endemic areas) and early treatment of primary infection in high-risk patients.

faqs.question: What should I do if I live in an endemic area?
faqs.answer: If you live in an endemic area (Southwestern US), be aware of coccidioidomycosis risk. Use respiratory protection (N95 mask) when working in dusty soil (construction, gardening). Stay indoors during dust storms. If you develop prolonged cough, fever, or fatigue, especially after soil disturbance, see a doctor and mention your geographic location — coccidioidomycosis should be considered.

faqs.question: What monitoring is needed during treatment?
faqs.answer: During treatment, monitor: symptoms (cough, fever, weight, hemoptysis) monthly; liver function tests every 3 months (azole antifungals are hepatotoxic); serum antifungal levels (for fluconazole at high doses, voriconazole, itraconazole) to ensure therapeutic levels; chest imaging every 6–12 months; complement fixation titers every 3–6 months to track response.
